---
title: "Module 3: Automated Harmonization"
execute: 
  enabled: false
---

## Step 3.1: Joint embedding training with BONMI

The Block-wise Overlapping Noisy Matrix Integration [(BONMI)](BONMI_package.qmd) algorithm helps combine data from different sources by aligning and completing SPPMI (Pointwise Mutual Information) matrices.

It populates this matrix by averaging SPPMI values from contributing institutions when overlaps exist and imputing missing values using orthogonal transformations. This approach enables the creation of a shared data representation, facilitating collaborative analysis across diverse datasets.

::: {.callout-note}
The following code examples in this part are written using the R programming language.
:::

Here we use the CSV files which are the output from Module 1 Step 4. The following script performs necessary preprocessing steps for BONMI analysis.

```{r}
# Load libraries
library(dplyr)      
library(nlpembeds)  
library(kgraph)     

# Set file path and load CSV files
folder_path <- "./step4_rolledup_finaldata/step4_rolledup_finaldata/Diagnoses"
csv_files <- list.files(path = folder_path, pattern = "*.csv", full.names = TRUE)

# Initialize variables
W = list()
s = 0
r = 350

# Process each CSV file: load data, modify structure, and compute SPPMI
for(x in csv_files) {
  s = s + 1
  input_df = read.csv(x)
  input_df$count <- 1
  input_df <- input_df[ , c(2,1,3,4)]
  colnames(input_df) <- c("Month", "Patient", "Parent_Code", "Count")
  spm_cooc = build_df_cooc(input_df)
  m_pmi = get_pmi(spm_cooc)
  W[[s]] = m_pmi
}

# Apply the BONMI algorithm
Xhat <- BONMI(W, r, weights=NULL, rsvd.use=FALSE)

# Fit embeddings and evaluate using ROC
set.seed(1)
fit_kg_bonmi = fit_embeds_kg(Xhat, 'cosine', df_pairs = df_known_pairs[c(1, 3)])
pROC::plot.roc(fit_kg_bonmi$roc, print.auc = TRUE)
print(fit_kg_bonmi$roc)
```

![](BONMI_AUC.png)

```
Call:
roc.default(response = truth, predictor = sims, direction = "<",     quiet = TRUE)

Data: sims in 2598 controls (truth 0) < 2598 cases (truth 1).
Area under the curve: 0.7915
```

The output of this process includes the embedding matrix and evaluation results from BONMI.

## Step 3.2: Joint embedding validation

BONMI embeddings can be improved by combining them with embeddings from pre-trained language models (PLMs). While BONMI focuses on aligning data from different sources, PLMs capture deeper meanings from text. Putting them together creates stronger embeddings, which are helpful for tasks like organizing and standardizing clinical data.

::: {.callout-note}
The following code examples in this part are written using the R programming language.
:::

Here we use the output from Module 2 Section C, `nlpembedding`, as one of the input data.

```{r}
# Convert embeddings and predictions to data frames
df_nlp_embedding <- as.data.frame(nlpembedding)
df_bonmi <- as.data.frame(Xhat)

df_nlp_embedding$RowNames <- rownames(df_nlp_embedding)
df_bonmi$RowNames <- rownames(df_bonmi)

# Merge data frames by row names
df_bonmi_merged <- merge(df_nlp_embedding, df_bonmi, by="RowNames", all=FALSE)

# Convert to matrix and assign row names
m_bonmi_merged <- as.matrix(df_bonmi_merged[, -1])
rownames(m_bonmi_merged) <- df_bonmi_merged$RowNames

# Fit embeddings to a knowledge graph
fit_kg_comb <- fit_embeds_kg(m_bonmi_merged, 'cosine', df_pairs = df_known_pairs[c(1, 3)])

# Plot and print ROC curve
pROC::plot.roc(fit_kg_comb$roc, print.auc = TRUE)
print(fit_kg_comb$roc)
```

![](nlp_BONMI.png)

```
Call:
roc.default(response = truth, predictor = sims, direction = "<",     quiet = TRUE)

Data: sims in 2598 controls (truth 0) < 2598 cases (truth 1).
Area under the curve: 0.8705
```

The output of this process includes the embedding matrix and evaluation results.

The AUC for the combined BONMI and NLP embeddings is 0.8705, showing stronger performance compared to the embeddings generated in Module 2 (AUC = 0.7746). This improvement demonstrates that combining the two methods enhances the ability to distinguish co-occurring PheCode pairs from non-co-occurring ones.
